discriminators: BIRDS as a CATEGORY is signal_narrowable: bird vocalization is a genuinely measurable class (high f0, typically 1-8 kHz, narrowband FM chirps, very fast pitch/centroid modulation, almost no energy below 200 Hz) and is reliably separable from a dog, an engine or an impact. The SUBCATEGORIES are species taxonomy and mostly are not.

This subcategory is BIMODAL and therefore not narrowable by a single Gaussian: a hawk or eagle screech is a harsh, broadband, high-flatness scream, while an owl hoot is the opposite - a low (300-600 Hz), pure, near-sinusoidal tone with very little HF energy. Averaging them produces priors that describe neither. Marked semantic_only for that reason, and the pitch prior is deliberately weak and wide.
An owl hoot is the one bird call that can be mistaken for a low tonal drone or a blown-bottle tone; the bird class gates barely hold it.
Against BIRDSong: a raptor screech is noisy (high spectral_flatness, low harmonicity); a songbird is tonal (the inverse). That axis is real - see BIRDCrow and BIRDSong.

BIRDS as a CATEGORY is signal_narrowable: bird vocalization is a genuinely measurable class (high f0, typically 1-8 kHz, narrowband FM chirps, very fast pitch/centroid modulation, almost no energy below 200 Hz) and is reliably separable from a dog, an engine or an impact. The SUBCATEGORIES are species taxonomy and mostly are not.

Corvids carry a real, defensible acoustic axis: a caw is a HARSH, BROADBAND, inharmonic rasp - high spectral_flatness, low harmonicity - delivered in repeated, evenly spaced bursts (measurable onset_periodicity). That is the exact opposite of a songbird's pure narrowband whistle, and the flatness/harmonicity axis separates the two without any text. Hence signal_narrowable.
It does NOT separate a crow from a raptor screech or a parrot squawk, which are equally harsh - those need text.
Pitch is low FOR A BIRD (roughly 0.5-1.5 kHz), which is another honest nudge against BIRDSong.

measured_evidence.selection: clips whose FSD50K labels map unambiguously to this one subcategory (high/medium crosswalk confidence only)

discriminators: BIRDS as a CATEGORY is signal_narrowable: bird vocalization is a genuinely measurable class (high f0, typically 1-8 kHz, narrowband FM chirps, very fast pitch/centroid modulation, almost no energy below 200 Hz) and is reliably separable from a dog, an engine or an impact. The SUBCATEGORIES are species taxonomy and mostly are not.

Landfowl, waterfowl and poultry - a habitat/husbandry set, not an acoustic one. A duck quack, a hen cluck, a rooster crow and a goose honk share nothing measurable that excludes the other bird subcategories. semantic_only; the synonyms decide.

Weak tendency: fowl calls are lower and harsher than a songbird (clucks and quacks are rough, mid-band, repeated), landing them nearer BIRDCrow than BIRDSong on the flatness axis. That tendency cannot tell a duck from a crow.
Farm MAMMALS go to ANIMALS-FARM; a dense barnyard bed is AMBIENCE-FARM.

explanation: Bird sounds not fitting another category in this list.

discriminators: BIRDS as a CATEGORY is signal_narrowable: bird vocalization is a genuinely measurable class (high f0, typically 1-8 kHz, narrowband FM chirps, very fast pitch/centroid modulation, almost no energy below 200 Hz) and is reliably separable from a dog, an engine or an impact. The SUBCATEGORIES are species taxonomy and mostly are not.

The catch-all: it asserts only the BIRD class (it is a bird, not a mammal or a machine) and leaves the species to text. Correct destination when the bird class gates pass but no sibling's synonyms fire - i.e. the abstention target for this category.

Woodpecker material is the odd one out: a drum roll is a rhythmic IMPACT train with no vocal pitch at all, and it will fail these tonal priors. Score it as a rhythmic wood impact if the signal is what matters.

explanation: Birds that live mostly near the ocean. Seagulls

discriminators: BIRDS as a CATEGORY is signal_narrowable: bird vocalization is a genuinely measurable class (high f0, typically 1-8 kHz, narrowband FM chirps, very fast pitch/centroid modulation, almost no energy below 200 Hz) and is reliably separable from a dog, an engine or an impact. The SUBCATEGORIES are species taxonomy and mostly are not.

'Birds that live mostly near the ocean' is a habitat label. A gull cry is a harsh, mid-high, strongly modulated wail; that is not distinguishable from a tern, a crow or a raptor by DSP. semantic_only.

Beware the recording context rather than the bird: seabird material usually arrives over a surf bed, so the file may score partly as WATER/AMBIENCE. Foregrounded calls belong here; a wide, stationary surf-plus-gulls bed is AMBIENCE.

discriminators: BIRDS as a CATEGORY is signal_narrowable: bird vocalization is a genuinely measurable class (high f0, typically 1-8 kHz, narrowband FM chirps, very fast pitch/centroid modulation, almost no energy below 200 Hz) and is reliably separable from a dog, an engine or an impact. The SUBCATEGORIES are species taxonomy and mostly are not.

Songbirds carry the one genuinely diagnostic axis inside BIRDS: their calls are PURE NARROWBAND WHISTLES - very high harmonicity, very low spectral_flatness, few partials (often a near-sinusoid, since the syrinx suppresses harmonics) - swept fast in frequency, which shows up as a large spectral_centroid_deviation_hz and high spectral_flux. The harsh-broadband siblings (BIRDCrow, BIRDPrey, BIRDTrop) sit at the opposite end of the same axis. That is a real measurement, so signal_narrowable.
It does NOT tell a robin from a wren from a warbler; species is text.
A dawn chorus of many overlapping birds is AMBIENCE, not this: expect high stationarity and no foreground event structure.

explanation: jungle and tropical rainforest birds. Parrots and Toucans.

discriminators: BIRDS as a CATEGORY is signal_narrowable: bird vocalization is a genuinely measurable class (high f0, typically 1-8 kHz, narrowband FM chirps, very fast pitch/centroid modulation, almost no energy below 200 Hz) and is reliably separable from a dog, an engine or an impact. The SUBCATEGORIES are species taxonomy and mostly are not.

'Birds living in tropical locations' is a geographic label with no acoustic content whatsoever - it contains both the harshest calls in the category (macaw and cockatoo squawks: broadband, high-flatness, screaming) and some of the purest (trogons, sunbirds). Bimodal and geography-defined, so semantic_only with weak, wide priors. Text is the only route in.
A parrot squawk is essentially indistinguishable from a corvid caw on flatness/harmonicity; a kookaburra is a rhythmic laugh that reads like a monkey call. Do not assert from signal.

explanation: long-billed birds that feed primarily in shallow water. Think Herons and Egrets.

discriminators: BIRDS as a CATEGORY is signal_narrowable: bird vocalization is a genuinely measurable class (high f0, typically 1-8 kHz, narrowband FM chirps, very fast pitch/centroid modulation, almost no energy below 200 Hz) and is reliably separable from a dog, an engine or an impact. The SUBCATEGORIES are species taxonomy and mostly are not.

Defined by anatomy and feeding habitat (long legs, shallow water) - about as far from an acoustic definition as UCS gets. A heron's croak, a crane's bugle and a sandpiper's peep have no common signature. semantic_only.

One notable outlier that strains the bird class gates: a bittern's boom is ~150-200 Hz with almost all energy in the low band, and reads as a low tonal drone rather than a bird. Expect the class gates, not the priors, to be the weak point there.
